message.content:
{
  "homework": {
    "sessionInformation": {
      "sessionNumber": "1",
      "sessionTitle": "Solutions - Session 1",
      "subject": "Chemistry",
      "grade": "Class XII / 12",
      "difficultyLevel": "Balanced",
      "learningPace": "Balanced",
      "estimatedHomeworkDuration": "45 minutes"
    },
    "homework": [
      {
        "id": 1,
        "type": "Conceptual Explanation & Real-World Application",
        "title": "Understanding the Nature of Solutions",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_01"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Definition and Types of Solutions", "Components: Solute and Solvent"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "8 minutes",
        "instructions": "Define a solution. Identify the solute and solvent in three different everyday examples (e.g., soda water, brass, air). Explain why these are classified as solutions.",
        "question": "1. Define 'Solution'.\n2. For each of the following mixtures, identify the Solute and Solvent:\na) Soda Water\nb) Brass (an alloy)\nc) Air\n3. Briefly explain how you determined which component is the solute in a gaseous solution like air.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Written response",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Definition: A homogeneous mixture of two or more substances.\nExamples:\na) Solute: CO2, Solvent: Water\nb) Solute: Zinc (or Copper depending on context), Solvent: Copper (base metal)\nc) Solute: Oxygen/CO2/Noble gases, Solvent: Nitrogen"
      },
      {
        "id": 2,
        "type": "Classification & Matching",
        "title": "Types of Solutions Based on States of Matter",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_01"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Eight Types of Solutions (Solid/Liquid/Gas solute in Solid/Liquid/Gas solvent)"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 6,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes",
        "instructions": "Complete the table below by providing a suitable example for each combination of Solute and Solvent states. Ensure you cover all eight types.",
        "question": "Fill in the missing examples or identify the type:\n\n| Type | Solute State | Solvent State | Example |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 | Gas | Liquid | Carbonated water (CO2 in H2O) |\n| 2 | Solid | Liquid | Saltwater |\n| 3 | Liquid | Liquid | Ethanol in Water |\n| 4 | Gas | Gas | Air |\n| 5 | Solid | Solid | Brass (Cu-Zn alloy) |\n| 6 | Liquid | Solid | Amalgam of Mercury with Sodium |\n| 7 | Solid | Gas | Camphor vaporized in Nitrogen |\n| 8 | Liquid | Gas | Water mist in Air",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Table completion",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "1. CO2 (Gas) / H2O (Liquid)\n2. NaCl (Solid) / H2O (Liquid)\n3. Ethanol (Liquid) / Water (Liquid)\n4. O2/N2 (Gas) / N2 (Gas)\n5. Zn/Cu (Solid) / Cu/Zn (Solid)\n6. Na/Li (Liquid/Solid depending on temp, usually Mercury is liquid solute in solid metal matrix or vice versa - e.g., Sodium amalgam: Hg(Liquid)/Na(Solid))\n7. Camphor (Solid) / Nitrogen (Gas)\n8. Water droplets (Liquid) / Air (Gas)"
      },
      {
        "id": 3,
        "type": "Numerical Problem",
        "title": "Calculating Mass Percentage and Mole Fraction",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_02"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Concentration Terms: Mass %, Mole Fraction"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 8,
        "estimatedTime": "15 minutes",
        "instructions": "Solve the following numerical problems. Show all steps clearly including formulas used.\n\nProblem A: Calculate the mass percentage of benzene (C6H6) and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) if 22 g of benzene is dissolved in 122 g of CCl4.\n\nProblem B: Calculate the mole fraction of benzene in a solution containing 30% by mass in cyclohexane.",
        "question": "A. A solution contains 22g of Benzene (C6H6) and 122g of Carbon Tetrachloride (CCl4). Calculate the % by mass of each component.\nB. A solution is prepared with a composition of 30% benzene by mass in cyclohexane. Calculate the mole fraction of benzene.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Show calculations",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "A.\nTotal Mass = 22 + 122 = 144g\n% Benzene = (22/144) * 100 = 15.28%\n% CCl4 = (122/144) * 100 = 84.72%\nB.\nAssume 100g solution: Benzene=30g, Cyclohexane=70g\nMolar Mass Benzene (C6H6) = 78 g/mol; Moles = 30/78 ≈ 0.385 mol\nMolar Mass Cyclohexane (C6H12) = 84 g/mol; Moles = 70/84 ≈ 0.833 mol\nTotal moles = 0.385 + 0.833 = 1.218 mol\nMole Fraction Benzene = 0.385 / 1.218 ≈ 0.316"
      },
      {
        "id": 4,
        "type": "Diagram Labelling & Conceptual Analysis",
        "title": "Solubility and Henry's Law Concepts",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_02"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Factors affecting solubility (Temperature/Pressure)", "Henry's Law Introduction"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 6,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes",
        "instructions": "Answer the following conceptual questions based on Henry's Law and general solubility trends.\n\n1. State Henry's Law regarding gas solubility in liquids.\n2. Explain why carbonated drinks are bottled under high pressure using Henry's Law.\n3. Describe how temperature affects the solubility of gases vs solids.",
        "question": "1. Write the mathematical expression for Henry's Law and define all terms.\n2. Why does a soda bottle fizz when opened?\n3. Compare the effect of increasing temperature on the solubility of (a) Oxygen in water, and (b) Sugar in water.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Written response",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "1. p = KH * x (where p is partial pressure, KH is Henry's constant, x is mole fraction).\n2. Opening reduces pressure; solubility decreases according to Henry's Law, causing CO2 gas to escape.\n3. Solubility of gases generally decreases with increasing temperature (exothermic dissolution). Solubility of solids like sugar generally increases with increasing temperature."
      },
      {
        "id": 5,
        "type": "Parent Engagement / Real-World Observation",
        "title": "Observation: Effervescence and Pressure",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_02"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Real-world application of Henry's Law"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "6 minutes (plus observation time)",
        "instructions": "Optional Activity: With a parent or guardian, observe an open soda bottle. Note the sound and bubbles released.\n\nTask:\n1. Describe what happens when you shake the bottle before opening it vs not shaking it.\n2. Discuss with your family why this phenomenon occurs based on today's lesson.",
        "question": "Observation Task: Open a sealed carbonated drink (soda/cola). \na) What do you hear and see immediately upon opening?\nb) If the bottle was shaken vigorously before opening, how does the reaction change? Explain using Henry's Law concepts discussed in class.\nc) Ask your parent/guardian if they have ever noticed this effect with other carbonated beverages or medicines.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Observation log",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "a) Hissing sound and rapid bubbling due to pressure drop.\nb) Shaking increases the surface area of gas bubbles, causing a more violent release (fizzing over) when pressure is released. This demonstrates that higher agitation + lower pressure = faster escape of dissolved gas."
      }
    ],
    "summary": {
      "totalQuestions": 5,
      "totalMarks": 30,
      "estimatedCompletionTime": "49 minutes",
      "learningOutcomesCovered": [
        "Define solutions and identify solute/solvent components.",
        "Classify solutions based on the physical states of solutes and solvents."
      ],
      "topicsCovered": [
        "Definition and Types of Solutions",
        "Concentration Terms (Mass %, Mole Fraction)",
        "Henry's Law"
      ],
      "subtopicsCovered": [
        "Eight types of solutions examples",
        "Calculations involving mass percentage",
        "Calculations involving mole fraction",
        "Effect of temperature and pressure on solubility"
      ],
      "taskDistribution": {
        "Conceptual Explanation & Real-World Application": 1,
        "Classification & Matching": 1,
        "Numerical Problem": 2,
        "Diagram Labelling & Conceptual Analysis": 1,
        "Parent Engagement / Real-World Observation": 1
      },
      "homeExperimentIncluded": false,
      "parentEngagementIncluded": true
    }
  }
}
